trivia_pieces: Dark Mode: not just for edgelords. It actually saves battery on OLED screens.
OLED pixels emit their own light. Black pixels are just... off. Zero power used.
On an LCD screen, dark mode just makes the backlight work harder. Useless.
The more black pixels, the less power consumed. Simple physics.
It's a win-win: looks cool and saves your phone's life.
The obsession is justified by science, not just aesthetics.
Some developers even claim it reduces eye strain. Possibly.
The trend started with developers, then spread to the masses.
So next time you enable dark mode, tell people it's for science.
The future is dark, and it's battery-efficient.

topic: The origin of the "CamelCase" naming convention
trivia_pieces: CamelCase: PascalCase, camelCase. Looks like a camel's humps, but less useful for desert travel.
It's a way to make multi-word identifiers readable without spaces.
No one knows the exact origin, but it's been around since the 1970s.
Used extensively in languages like Java and C#. Makes code look... hump-y.
The opposite of snake_case. Choose your allegiance.
It's a convention, not a rule. But break it, and you'll anger the code gods.
The name itself is a bit of a pun on the visual appearance.
PascalCase capitalizes the first letter, camelCase doesn't. The devil's in the details.
It avoids the need for spaces, which can be problematic in some programming contexts.
Basically, it's a way to cram words together elegantly.

topic: Why "Markdown" was invented by a blogger
trivia_pieces: Markdown: invented by John Gruber, a blogger, to make writing for the web easier.
The goal was to create a simple, plain-text syntax that could be converted to HTML.
It's the antithesis of verbose markup languages. Simplicity wins.
Now used everywhere from README files to chat apps. The ultimate democratizer of text.
It proves that sometimes the best tools come from people who actually need them.
The syntax is so intuitive, it feels like you're just writing normally.
The 'why' was crucial: to allow people to write using an easy-to-read, easy-to-write plain text format.
It's the digital equivalent of using shorthand.
From blog posts to documentation, Markdown conquered the web with its elegance.
It's the duct tape of text formatting, but way cleaner.

topic: The history of the "Trollface" in early web forums
trivia_pieces: Trollface originated on 4chan in 2008, proving rage comics were peak internet art.
Its creator, Carlos Ramirez, made $0 from it, a true testament to early internet freedom.
The face basically says 'I'm here to annoy you, and I'm good at it.'
Before memes were memes, Trollface was a digital harbinger of online chaos.
It survived the death of MS Paint comics, a true digital cockroach.
Some say the eyebrows hold the secrets to infinite comment section arguments.
It's the OG 'problem?' face, before you even knew you had a problem.
From 4chan to your mom's Facebook, Trollface has seen it all... and judged it.
It's the digital equivalent of sticking your tongue out, but with more existential dread.
Proof that a simple MS Paint drawing can launch a thousand bad jokes.

topic: Why "Binary" isn't the only way (Ternary computers)
trivia_pieces: Ternary logic uses three states: 0, 1, and 'what if we tried this?'
Soviet scientists actually built ternary computers. They were just ahead of their time (or confused).
Think of it as a dimmer switch for your data. Much more nuanced.
Balanced ternary offers negative values without a sign bit. Mind. Blown.
It's like binary's cooler, more sophisticated cousin who always has a better story.
Why settle for ON/OFF when you can have ON/OFF/MAYBE?
Some argue ternary is more efficient. Others argue about compiler optimizations instead.
The SETUN computer was a real ternary machine. Probably ran on strong tea and existentialism.
It's proof that not all computing needs to be a binary choice.
Imagine debugging ternary code. It’s like trying to find a bug in a Schrödinger's cat.
The term 'hackathon' was coined independently by Sun Microsystems and the Hackerspace community in 1999.
It's a portmanteau of 'hack' (in the exploratory programming sense) and 'marathon'.
So, it's basically a test of endurance for people who can't sit still.
The original goal was to get developers to build open-source software quickly.
Now it's mostly about free pizza, caffeine, and bragging rights.
Some hackathons are so intense, they require sleep deprivation permits.
The 'hack' doesn't usually involve breaking into systems, thankfully.
It's like speed dating for code, but with more energy drinks.
The earliest ones were likely fueled by pure passion and questionable pizza toppings.
Think of it as a pressure cooker for innovation, or just a very loud office.
Linus Torvalds initially wanted to call it 'Freax' (free, freak, ex).
Thankfully, someone pointed out that 'Freax' sounded like 'fart' in some languages.
The FTP server owner thought 'Linux' was better. They weren't wrong.
Imagine the t-shirts: 'I run Freax!' Not quite as cool.
It could have been the OS where all the bugs were 'freaks'.
Linus reportedly wanted to avoid sounding too arrogant. Too late.
The name 'Linux' is technically a mispronunciation. Even better.
So, we dodged a bullet of potentially awkward naming conventions.
The world's most popular open-source kernel was almost named after a bodily function.
Let's just be grateful it wasn't 'Linus's Operating System Thingy'.

topic: The history of the "Internet of Things" toaster
trivia_pieces: The first 'smart' toaster was showcased in 1990, proving IoT has always been about convenience (and absurdity).
It likely connected via dial-up. Toasting took longer than baking.
Imagine the security risks: someone remotely burning your toast.
The early IoT toaster was less about data, more about 'can we plug this thing in?'
It predicted a future where your breakfast appliance spies on your Wi-Fi.
We've come a long way from 'toast level 3' to 'toast level 3 via blockchain'.
The goal was likely to avoid getting out of bed to toast bread. Ambitious.
It's a relic of a time when 'connected' meant 'makes annoying beeping sounds'.
Perhaps it was the first step towards sentient kitchen appliances.
Honestly, who needs their toaster online? Apparently, everyone who can afford it.
JSON is basically JavaScript Object Notation, meaning it's human-readable and less verbose.
XML looks like it was designed by someone who enjoys typing `<` and `>` repeatedly.
Parsing JSON is faster and simpler for most web applications.
JSON is like the minimalist designer of data formats; XML is the maximalist.
Think of XML as needing a whole stylesheet just to say 'hello'. JSON just says 'hello'.
APIs needed something lightweight to avoid sending gigabytes of angle brackets.
JSON won because developers are inherently lazy (and efficient).
It's the difference between a concise tweet and a rambling novel.
XML's verbosity made mobile development a pain. JSON offered sweet relief.
JSON's simplicity made it the de facto standard for web services. Who knew?
Heartbleed wasn't a malicious hack, but a bug in OpenSSL's implementation of TLS/DTLS.
It allowed attackers to read up to 64KB of memory from affected servers.
Essentially, servers would give away little pieces of their secrets when asked nicely (and repeatedly).
The name 'Heartbleed' comes from 'Heartbeat', the TLS extension where the bug lived.
This vulnerability existed for about two years before anyone noticed. Oops.
It exposed private keys, passwords, and other sensitive data. Fun times.
The fix was surprisingly simple: add a missing bounds check. Developers everywhere facepalmed.
It was a stark reminder that even foundational security software can have critical flaws.
Imagine forgetting to close a parenthesis and accidentally leaking the internet's secrets.
The bug was so simple, it felt like finding a typo in the source code of the universe.

topic: Why "TypeScript" is just JavaScript with a safety vest
trivia_pieces: TypeScript adds static typing to JavaScript, like adding guardrails to a skateboard ramp.
It catches errors during development, not when your users are yelling.
Basically, it's JavaScript that decided to take its vitamins and wear a helmet.
You can still write terrible code in TypeScript, but it's harder.
It compiles down to plain JavaScript, so browsers don't need a TypeScript interpreter.
Think of it as JavaScript's slightly more responsible older sibling.
It helps manage large codebases by forcing you to define what things are.
The type system is optional, which is why some developers still prefer pure chaos.
It's like having a linting tool that actually knows what it's talking about.
TypeScript: Because 'undefined is not a function' is a terrible runtime error.

topic: The history of the "Blue Screen of Death" (BSOD)
trivia_pieces: The BSOD has been a Windows staple since Windows 3.1, a true OG.
It signifies a critical system error that Windows can't recover from.
Early BSODs were less informative, more 'YOU SCREWED UP. GOODBYE.'
The iconic blue color was chosen for readability against white text.
It's the digital equivalent of a presidential impeachment: the system is fundamentally broken.
Many BSODs are caused by faulty hardware or bad drivers. The usual suspects.
The specific error codes are often cryptic puzzles for IT professionals.
It's the ultimate 'Ctrl+Alt+Delete' prerequisite.
Some believe the BSOD is a sentient entity judging our coding practices.
Survived by generations of Windows users, it's a rite of passage.

topic: Why "Agile" started in a ski resort in Utah
trivia_pieces: The Agile Manifesto was signed in Snowbird, Utah, by 17 software developers.
They met to discuss lightweight development methods, possibly fueled by mountain air and existential dread.
The location choice was probably less about skiing and more about escaping the 'corporate ski slope' of waterfall development.
It was a rebellion against heavy, rigid documentation and processes.
They wanted faster, more flexible software development, not faster ski runs (though that was a perk).
The 'Utah Accord' sounds more like a treaty than a software manifesto.
The goal was to value individuals and interactions over processes and tools.
Basically, they wanted to build software like humans, not like robots assembling cars.
The irony is that 'Agile' itself has become a process that some companies over-engineer.
From a snowy mountain top, a revolution in software development was born. And maybe some good après-ski stories.
